coredata.dc:title: Performance improvement of dye sensitized solar cells based on cadmium sulfide/S, N co doped carbon dots nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Safardoust-Hojaghan, Hossein
Amiri, Omid
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Hassanpour, Mohammad
Khojasteh, Hossein
Foong, Loke Kok
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, cadmium sulfide (CdS), sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) and cadmium sulfide/sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (CdS/S,N-CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal route. Cadmium acetate dihydrate, thiourea and citric acid were applied as a source of cadmium, sulfur and carbon respectively. Thiourea plays dual role as a dopant source for doping of carbon dots and sulfur source for the synthesis of cadmium sulfide. The influence of hydrothermal temperature on the morphology of CdS and CdS/S,N-CDs was determined in terms of structure, size and morphology through X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis. Results showed that temperature has a significant effect on the morphology of cadmium sulfide. Homogenous flower like morphology of CdS in 160 °C was converted to non-homogenous worm-like particles at 200 °C. Moreover, the flower-like morphology of CdS was converted to the particle morphology during the preparation of CdS/S,N-CDs since the acidity of solution is changed for the presence of citric acid. After morphological and structural engineering, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and CdS/S,N-CDs were applied in dye sensitized solar cells. The JSC of S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-CDs were measured 14.07 and 18.38 mA/cm2 respectively. Results showed that the VOC equal to 0.61 V for CDs while decreased to 0.59 V in CdS/S,N-CDs case. Although VOC decreased, hole PCE show improvement in CdS/S,N-CDs case, PCE enhanced from 3.44% to 5.7%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Solar cell
Flower-like morphology
Carbon dots
Hydrothermal

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of as prepared a) CdS b) CdS/S,N-CdS at 160 °C and 10 h.
FT-IR spectrum of as prepared a) CdS b) CdS/S,N-CdS at 160 °C and 10 h.
SEM images of prepared flower-like CdS at 160 °C and 10 h with different magnifications.
SEM images of prepared disordered flower-like CdS at 180 °C and 10 h with different magnifications.
SEM images of prepared particle morphology of CdS at 200 °C and 10 h with different magnifications.
TEM images of prepared flower-like CdS at 160 °C and 10 h with different magnifications.
SEM images of prepared spherical morphology of CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites at 160 °C and 10 h with different magnifications.
TEM images of prepared CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites at 160 °C and 10 h with different magnifications.
a) EDS analysis of prepared CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites at 160 °C and 10 h b) EDS analysis of prepared S,N-CDs at 160 °C and 10 h c) UV–Vis spectrum of S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites.
Photocurrent density–voltage characteristics of DSSCs based on S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-CDs.
The photovoltaic parameters for DSSCs based S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-CdS nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Performance improvement of dye sensitized solar cells based on cadmium sulfide/S, N co doped carbon dots nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Soran University, PO Box 624, Soran, Kurdistan Regional Government, Iraq

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Soran University, PO Box 624, Soran, Kurdistan Regional Government, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Research and Development, Duy Tan University, Da Nang 550000, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Research and Development, Duy Tan University, Da Nang, 550000, Viet Nam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, cadmium sulfide (CdS), sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) and cadmium sulfide/sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (CdS/S,N-CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal route. Cadmium acetate dihydrate, thiourea and citric acid were applied as a source of cadmium, sulfur and carbon respectively. Thiourea plays dual role as a dopant source for doping of carbon dots and sulfur source for the synthesis of cadmium sulfide. The influence of hydrothermal temperature on the morphology of CdS and CdS/S,N-CDs was determined in terms of structure, size and morphology through X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis. Results showed that temperature has a significant effect on the morphology of cadmium sulfide. Homogenous flower like morphology of CdS in 160 °C was converted to non-homogenous worm-like particles at 200 °C. Moreover, the flower-like morphology of CdS was converted to the particle morphology during the preparation of CdS/S,N-CDs since the acidity of solution is changed for the presence of citric acid. After morphological and structural engineering, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and CdS/S,N-CDs were applied in dye sensitized solar cells. The JSC of S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-CDs were measured 14.07 and 18.38 mA/cm2 respectively. Results showed that the VOC equal to 0.61 V for CDs while decreased to 0.59 V in CdS/S,N-CDs case. Although VOC decreased, hole PCE show improvement in CdS/S,N-CDs case, PCE enhanced from 3.44% to 5.7%.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy is at the center of all human action and there is a nearby interface between the accessibility of vitality and the development of a country [1,2]. Solar cell is an excellent possibility for direct transformation of daylight to power since it is basically boundless and has negligible contamination or physical risk. From 1950, when the first solar cell was designed to now, solar cells suffer from generous confinements such as high cost and low efficiency [3–5]. So, many investigations have been done to resolve these limitations. In past decades, different nanostructures have been applied to overcome these challenges [6–8].

Due to their unique thermoelectric, optical and semiconducting properties, different transition metal sulfides nanostructures (TMSN) have been synthesized and applied in the improvement of solar cells [9–13]. Till now, various hybrids of TMS have been prepared and applied in solar cell field. Hue Shen and et al. used cadmium sulfide for fabrication of heterojunction solar cells. They studied the different parameters such as the thickness and evaporation rate of cadmium sulfide, properties of Al and hydrogen co-doped ZnO layer, and the presence of MoO3 thin films on the efficiency of CdS/p-Si solar cells and 10.64% was obtained as optimized efficiency [14]. Xing Qian and et al. prepared cobalt iron selenide/sulfide nanocubes as counter electrode materials for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Results showed that 9.58% efficiency was obtained via applying cobalt iron selenide/sulfide nanocubes as counter electrode which is higher than that of Pt CE (8.16%) [15]. Hee-Je Kim and et al. studied nickel sulfide (NiS) as a counter electrode for DSSCs. Designed DSSC with the nickel sulfide particles as the counter electrode was utilized a power conversion efficiency of 5.69% under sunlight illumination (100 mW cm−2, AM 1.5 G) [16].

Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of carbon-based nanostructures, have been recognized as separate, quasi-spherical particles with sizes beneath 10 nm [17–20]. CDs belong to zero-dimensional (0D) nanostructures and carbon atoms in it are arranged in sp2 hybridization. For their excellent properties, CDs have been applied in various fields. One of the attractive features of CDs is the synthesis-dependent properties. In another word, the characteristic of obtained carbon dots strongly depends on the applied procedure and precursors. Based on the applied method for preparation, CDs possess different properties [21–23]. Till now, various methods have been used for synthesis of CDs. In this case, according to the application field, all efforts have been focused on the preparation of CDs in low cost and eco-friend with considerable characteristics. There are two main routes for fabrication of CDs: 1) “top-down” that attributes synthesis of carbon dots from a different bulk carbon-based materials and 2) “bottom-up” that ascribe as synthesis from carbogenic source via manipulation of carbon atmos. For its intrinsic quality, the latter case is very interesting for researches. The bottom-up approach has notable ability to produce CDs via green sources [24–27].

In present work, different routes were applied for morphology and doping engineering of S,N-doped CDs/CdS nanohybrid. Prepared S-doped CDs (S-CDs), CdS and CdS/S,N-CDs are characterized by XRD, FT-IR, SEM and TEM analysis. Synthesized S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-CDs have been applied for DSSC and compared.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns analysis was done by a Philips-X'pertpro, X-ray diffractometer employing Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation. Nicolet Magna-550 spectrometer in KBr pellets was applied for recording Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. Morphological properties of products were investigated via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) that obtained on LEO-1455VP equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. For the in-depth investigation of morphological structure, Philips EM208S transmission electron microscope was used. GC-2550TG (Teif Gostar Faraz Company, Iran) was used for all chemical analyses. The entire chemicals used in this investigation were of analytical grade: Citric acid (CA) (99.5%), thiourea (TU) (99%), cadmium (II) acetate, P25, from Merck.

0.5 g Cd (CH3COO)2·2H2O was dissolved in 35 ml deionized water under stirrer. After the complete solution of Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O, transparent solution was formed. In this stage, 0.14 g thiourea (1:1 M ratio) was added to the solution under vigorous stirrer. After 30 min, the solution was transferred to 20 ml Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160, 180 and 200 °C for 10 h.

S,N-CDs was prepared according to our previous report [28]. Briefly, 1 g of CA was solubilized in 15 ml deionized water and 0.2 g thiourea was dissolved in 20 ml deionized water. The solution of thiourea was added to the solution of CA and stirred for 30 min at ambient condition. Then the as obtained solution was moved into 20 ml Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 10 h. The brownish solution was obtained as a final product by centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 20 min.

0.5 g Cd (CH3COO)2·2H2O was dissolved in 35 ml deionized water. 1 g cirtic acid was added to the solution and stirred to form a transparent solution. 0.5 g of thiourea was solubilized in as prepared solution. Obtained solution was transferred into 20 ml Teflon lined stainless autoclave at optimum temperature and time.

First, FTO glasses were washed by deionized water and acetone under ultrasonic three times. Then, thin film of TiO2 past deysol with average particle size of 20 nm was deposited on FTO glasses. Then deposited glasses was heated 60 min under 500 °C. TiO2 deposited glasses was immersed in prepared 1% (w/v) of CdS, S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-CDs in ethanol for 2 min. Samples were dried in 70 °C for 10 h. The fabricated electrodes were immersed in cis-di(thiocyanato)-N,N′-bis(2,20-bipyridyl-4-carboxylicacid-40-tetrabutylammonium carboxylate) ruthenium(II) (N-719).

XRD analysis was applied for determination of purity and crystallinity. Fig. 1 shows XRD pattern of prepared CdS and CdS/S,N-CDs. Results showed that cadmium sulfide formed hexagonal crystal system with space group of P63mc. Fig. 1a shows formation of CdS with any impurity. Fig. 2b that shows XRD pattern of CdS/S,N-CDs has some minor differences with XRD pattern of CdS. In CdS/S,N-CDs, peaks are broader than CdS. Broad of peaks in XRD pattern is extensively depend on shape and size of prepared CdS. So, results confirm size and shapes of cadmium sulfide are different in CdS and CdS/S,N-CDs. Scherrer equation was applied for calculation of crystalline size. The measured crystalline sizes from Scherrer equation for CdS and CdS/S,N-CDs were 34 and 22 nm, respectively [29].

Fig. 2 illustrates near-infrared spectroscopy of prepared CdS and CdS/S,N-CDs. Fig. 2a shows no related peak because CdS possess a broad absorption band in the far infrared region (range from 300 to 200 cm− 1). Fig. 2b shows infrared spectrum of CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposite. The mild band at 1010 cm− 1 is related to CO band that confirms presence of oxygen containing functional group on the surface of prepared carbon dots. Peak at 1668.18 cm− 1 confirms formation of carbonyl groups (CO), strongly. Peak at 1401.89 cm− 1 can be attributed to carbon‑carbon double bond (CC) stretching mode. As a result, FT-IR spectrum confirms formation of CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposite.

In hydrothermal process, the size and morphology of particles could be affected by the reaction temperature [30–40]. For this, three different temperature were applied for preparation of cadmium sulfide. SEM analysis was applied for investigation of temperature-related peculiarity. Fig. 3 shows SEM images of prepared sample at a temperature of 160 °C. Different magnifications of SEM images confirm the formation of uniform flower-like morphology. When temperature of process increases to 180 °C, disordered flower-like morphology is observed. Fig. 4 shows SEM images of prepared cadmium sulfide at 180 °C for 10 h in different magnifications. Crystal growth; nucleation and agglomeration during the hydrothermal process increase considerably when temperature rises [32]. This justification is confirmed completely as temperature rises to 200 °C. SEM images of prepared samples at 200 °C reveals formation of non-uniform and agglomerated particles. These worm-like nanostructures stick together and form larger particles (Fig. 5 ). From the SEM results, it is revealed that 160 °C and 10 h are the optimum temperature and time for the preparation of CdS respectively. TEM analysis was applied for in-depth investigation of morphology and size of particles. Fig. 6 shows TEM images of prepared CdS at 160 °C and 10 h. It is obvious that cadmium sulfide particles are arranged as flower-like morphology. Higher magnification of TEM images confirms this interesting arrangement clearly.

According to obtained results, CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites were prepared in-situ via hydrothermal route at 160 °C and 10 h. SEM images show that flower-like morphology of CdS is converted to the spherical morphology in CdS/S,N-CDs case. Fig. 7 shows the uniform spherical morphology of product. It can be attributed to the presence of citric acid as a precursor of carbon dots in the preparation of CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites. In the presence of citric acid, solution of cadmium ions and thiourea is acidified (pH = 5.7). Acidity plays key role in the formation of CdS/S,N-CDs and has strong effects on the morphology and particle size.

For the inherent limitation of SEM analysis, cadmium sulfide particles and carbon dots are not separable in the SEM images. TEM analysis was used to distinguish of cadmium sulfide particles and carbon dots in CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites (Fig. 8 ). TEM images in different magnification confirms formation of CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites.

EDS analysis was applied for determination of purity of product. EDS analysis of CdS/S,N-CDs, and S,N-CDs are presented in Fig. 9a and Fig. 9b respectively. It confirms presence of cadmium, carbon and sulfur elements in final products. It can be concluded that CdS/S,N-CDs has been prepared with no impurity. Fig. 9c shows UV–Vis spectrum of CdS/S,N-CDs and S,N-CDs. In the S,N-CDs case appeared absorption band at 306 nm is attributed to π-π* transition. By increasing of wavelength, absorbance of S,N-CDs decrease abruptly. CdS/S,N-CDs shows broad absorption peak at 310 nm. Absorbance of CdS/S,N-CDs is decreased gradually by increasing of wavelength.

There are few researches in application of carbon dots in the solar cells. CDs were applied as light-harvesters for mesoscopic solar cells by Qunwei Tang and et al. The solar cell tailored with CCQDs/N719 yields a maximized power conversion efficiency of 8.68% in comparison to 7.83% for N719-only device. Zhengbo Jiao and et al. obtained maximized power conversion efficiency as high as 9.29% under one sun illumination for N300-CDs/N719 co-sensitized DSSC in comparison with 8.09% for N300-CQDs-free device. Here we prepare S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-CDs nanocomposites and applied them in dye sensitized solar cells for the first time. According to the results, device based on S,N-CDs show the JSC of 14.07 mA/cm2. It also shows the VOC equal to 0.61 V. By using CdS/S,N-CDs composite as sensitizer, JSC has increased to 18.38 mA/cm2, while VOC decreased to 0.59 V. Although VOC decreased, hole PCE show improvement, PCE enhanced from 3.44% to 5.7%. The photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Fig. 10 and Table 1 .

In conclusion, cadmium sulfide (CdS), sulfur and nitrogen co doped carbon dots and cadmium sulfide/sulfur and nitrogen co doped carbon dots nanocomposite (CdS/S,N-CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal method. It is found that morphology of product can be affected by the different parameters. So, flower like and particle morphology of CdS and CdS/S,N-CDs were prepared respectively. Prepared S,N-CDs and CdS/S,N-GQDs were applied in dye sensitized solar cells. The JSC of S,N-CDS and CdS/S,N-CDs were calculated 14.07 and 18.38 mA/cm2 respectively. Results showed that the VOC equal to 0.61 V for S,N-CDs while decreased to 0.59 V in CdS/S,N-CDs case. Although VOC decreased, hole PCE shows improvement in CdS/S,N-CDs case, PCE enhanced from 3.44% to 5.7%.

Hossein Safardoust-Hojaghan: Software, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Omid Amiri: Visualization, Formal analysis.Masoud Salavati-Niasari: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration.Mohammad Hassanpour: Resources, Validation.Hossein Khojasteh: Investigation.Loke Kok Foong: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest regarding the publication of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/879).
